title: The shortest bar is your creature's closest relative
setup:
# --- plot helper (hidden) — your species_distance draws every bar ---
# Your creature's genome vs five animals in the zoo. Each bar is how far apart
# their DNA is. The SHORTEST bar (highlighted) is the closest relative.
def __plot(_):
    target = "ATCATCATC"
    zoo = [
        ("wolf", "GTCATCATC"),
        ("fox", "GGCATCATC"),
        ("bear", "GGGATCATC"),
        ("frog", "GGGGGCATC"),
        ("snake", "GGGGGGGGC"),
    ]
    dists = []
    for name, genome in zoo:
        try:
            d = species_distance(target, genome)          # <- the STUDENT's function
            if not isinstance(d, (int, float)):
                raise ValueError
        except Exception:
            d = sum(1 for a, b in zip(target, genome) if a != b)
        dists.append(d)
    shortest = min(dists)
    series = []
    for i, ((name, _genome), d) in enumerate(zip(zoo, dists)):
        x = i + 1
        series.append({
            "name": name + (" (closest!)" if d == shortest else ""),
            "points": [[x, 0], [x, d]],                     # a vertical bar
            "highlight": d == shortest,
        })
    return series

caption.todo: Finish species_distance to measure every creature and find the closest cousin.
caption.tuning: Your bars are growing — keep tuning species_distance so each one counts the real differences.
caption.match: ✅ The shortest bar wins — that's your creature's closest relative!
